params.text:
Unconscious bias

Not all unfairness is intentional. Unconscious bias describes the quick assumptions our brains make about people based on stereotypes – often without us realising. We all have biases; they come from our upbringing, culture and past experiences.

In care, unconscious bias can quietly shape decisions. You might assume an older person is hard of hearing, that someone who is quiet does not want to join in, or that a person of a certain faith would prefer a particular meal – without ever checking. Even well-meaning assumptions reduce a person's choice, control and dignity.

You cannot switch bias off, but you can manage it:

  • Notice when you are making an assumption – and pause.
  • Ask the person about their own preferences rather than guessing.
  • Get to know each individual as a unique human being.
  • Reflect honestly on your own practice and be open to feedback.

params.question: Because unconscious bias is unintentional, there is nothing a care worker can realistically do about it.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: That's not right. We can't switch bias off, but we can actively manage it by pausing, checking our assumptions and asking each person what they want.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. While we cannot eliminate bias, we can notice it, question our assumptions and ask people about their own preferences – which protects their dignity and choice.
